title: Why heat pumps are still so hot in the US
gist: US heat pump sales kept rising in early 2026 even after a $2,000 federal tax credit expired on January 1, 2026, a sharp contrast to EVs, whose sales collapsed once their federal tax credit lapsed in late 2025 — suggesting heat pump demand has become largely subsidy-independent.

insights.claim: US heat pump sales have doubled over the past 15 years and outpaced natural-gas furnace sales by 32% in the first quarter of 2026, according to a new industry report.
insights.speaker: Casey Crownhart
insights.so_what: Signals a durable structural shift in US home-heating equipment purchases toward electrification, relevant to HVAC manufacturers, utilities, and grid-electrification demand forecasts.
insights.quote: heat pump sales have doubled over the past 15 years, according to a new report. And they’re winning the heating race against fossil fuels, outpacing natural-gas furnaces by 32% during the first quarter of 2026.

insights.claim: A federal tax credit of up to $2,000 for US heat pump installations expired on January 1, 2026, yet heat pump shipment data (from the Air Conditioning, Heating, and Refrigeration Institute, which represents about 90% of the US market) show shipments were flat from December to January and then rose gradually into spring, a seasonal pattern that was slightly stronger in 2026 than in prior years.
insights.speaker: Casey Crownhart
entities: Air Conditioning, Heating, and Refrigeration Institute
insights.so_what: Demand held up through the exact window analysts would expect a subsidy-driven air pocket, indicating heat pump adoption is no longer materially dependent on federal incentives — a bullish read for HVAC electrification suppliers even in a less favorable policy environment.
insights.quote: Heat pump shipments were flat from December to January and have seen a gradual rise since then... This increase from winter into spring follows a seasonal trend seen in previous years—and it’s actually a bit stronger in 2026.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "Last year, though, the Trump administration slashed"

insights.claim: Energy economist Lucas Davis (UC Berkeley) concluded in a new analysis that the US heat pump market is strong enough not to depend on tax credits, based on post-expiration shipment data.
insights.speaker: Lucas Davis (cited by Casey Crownhart)
insights.so_what: An independent economist's data-backed conclusion that heat pump demand is subsidy-inelastic reduces policy risk for investors in heat pump manufacturers and installers going forward.
insights.quote: It appears that the U.S. market for heat pumps is strong enough that it does not depend on tax credits.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "This data isn’t what you’d expect"

insights.claim: When the federal EV tax credit of up to $7,500 expired on September 30, 2025, EV sales spiked in the preceding quarter as buyers rushed to claim it, then fell sharply afterward, showing EV demand was much more subsidy-dependent than heat pump demand has proven to be.
insights.speaker: Casey Crownhart
insights.so_what: A useful comparative case study: not all clean-technology categories respond the same way to subsidy removal — investors should model EV and heat pump policy sensitivity separately rather than assuming uniform 'clean tech' subsidy dependence.
insights.quote: Tax credits of up to $7,500 for new EVs ended on September 30, 2025. In the quarter leading up to that deadline, sales spiked as people rushed to take advantage of the incentive. Then they fell off a cliff.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "Last year, though, the Trump administration slashed"

insights.claim: Heat pumps have outsold gas furnaces in the US for four consecutive years, and countries including China and Germany have also seen strong recent growth in heat pump adoption.
insights.speaker: Casey Crownhart
insights.so_what: Points to a multi-country, multi-year decarbonization tailwind for heat pump component and equipment makers, not a US-only or subsidy-driven phenomenon.
insights.quote: The appliances have outsold gas furnaces in the US for the last four years... Countries including China and Germany have seen strong movement to heat pumps in recent years.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "In 2024, MIT Technology Review put heat pumps"

top_entities: Air Conditioning, Heating, and Refrigeration Institute
